region_of_origin: China (Cantonese tradition); widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Chinese double-boiled tonic soups — silkie (black-bone) chicken with wolfberries (goji berries) and red dates

ontology.foodon_label: chicken soup (closest parent — FoodOn does not have a silkie-specific or double-boiled entry)
ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for silkie (black-bone) chicken or double-boiled soup preparations. FOODON_03301521 (chicken soup) is used as the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken soup — closest FoodEx2 match; no silkie-specific or double-boiled code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for silkie chicken or double-boiled preparation. A0FJP (chicken soup) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to double-boiled silkie chicken soup as a food item.

preparation_method: slow-simmering in ceramic pot within a water bath

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one bowl (300 ml) containing both the broth and a portion of the silkie chicken meat, wolfberries, and red dates.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken soup, home-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted for silkie variety and wolfberry/red-date additions using HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for double-boiled silkie chicken soup with wolfberries and red dates exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy chicken soup (home-prepared, FDC ID 172152) with minor upward adjustments to protein and micronutrients (selenium, zinc, iron, B vitamins) to reflect silkie chicken's documented higher mineral density, and small carbohydrate/sugar additions from wolfberries and red dates. The resulting per-100g profile (40 kcal, 4.5g protein, 1.7g fat, 1.5g carbohydrate) is consistent with the HPB Foodo range for Chinese tonic soups and is internally coherent. Adaptation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chicken soup and broth-based soups — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate broth-based soup category; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for double-boiled silkie chicken soup with wolfberries and red dates. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous category of broth-based chicken soups and low-carbohydrate tonic soups in the University of Sydney GI Database. Total carbohydrate per 300 ml serving is approximately 4.5 g, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 1.8 — clinically negligible. The wolfberries and red dates contribute small amounts of simple sugars but do not materially elevate the GI of the overall dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (60 mg per 100 g; approximately 180 mg per 300 ml bowl)
hypertension.guidance: With only 180 mg sodium per standard bowl — well below the 600 mg per meal threshold recommended for hypertensive patients — this soup is an excellent choice for individuals managing blood pressure, provided no additional salt is added during preparation or at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 300 ml bowl) and negligible glycemic load (~1.8)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of approximately 1.8 per bowl is clinically negligible; this soup will not meaningfully raise postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal, with no portion restriction required based on glycemic impact alone.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g), low sodium, and high-quality lean protein from silkie chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content (1.5 g per bowl), very low sodium (180 mg per bowl), and lean complete protein profile make this soup a heart-healthy option; the wolfberries contribute zeaxanthin and beta-carotene with antioxidant properties relevant to vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory phytonutrients from wolfberries and selenium from silkie chicken
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Wolfberries provide zeaxanthin, beta-carotene, and polysaccharides with demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties in cell studies; selenium (6 mcg per 100 g) supports glutathione peroxidase activity, and carnosine and anserine from silkie chicken muscle contribute antioxidant activity — making this soup a supportive dietary choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double-Boiled Silkie Chicken Soup with Wolfberries and Red Dates (炖乌鸡汤，枸杞红枣) is a premium Chinese tonic soup rooted in Cantonese and Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) culinary tradition. It is prepared by slow-cooking silkie chicken — a breed with distinctive black skin, black bones, and black organs (烏骨雞, wū gǔ jī) — together with dried wolfberries (goji berries, 枸杞) and red dates (jujube, 红枣) using the double-boiling method: the ingredients are sealed in a ceramic pot which is then placed inside a larger pot of simmering water and cooked for 2–4 hours. This indirect heat method preserves heat-sensitive volatile aromatic compounds and produces a clear, golden broth with a delicate, mildly sweet savoury flavour and tender chicken pieces. Per 300 ml bowl, the soup delivers approximately 120 kcal, 13.5 g protein, 5.1 g fat, and only 4.5 g carbohydrate — making it nutritionally excellent for all chronic disease profiles. Key micronutrients include selenium (18 mcg per bowl), zinc, iron, B vitamins (B6, B12, niacin), vitamin A (from wolfberry carotenoids), and potassium. The glycemic load per bowl is negligible (~1.8), sodium is very low (~180 mg per bowl), and saturated fat is minimal. In Singapore, this soup is widely available at Chinese herbal medicine shops and TCM clinics, and is frequently recommended for postpartum recovery, post-surgical convalescence, elderly nutrition, and general immune support. The TCM 'qi-restoring' claim is not supported by RCT evidence, but the documented nutrient profile — particularly selenium, zinc, iron, and carotenoids — is genuinely supportive of immune function and general health. Clinically suitable for patients with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for double-boiled silkie chicken soup with wolfberries and red dates exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy chicken soup with adjustments for silkie variety and added ingredients. The per-100g macronutrient profile is internally coherent and consistent with the HPB Foodo range for Chinese tonic soups. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chicken soup, home-prepared, FDC ID 172152) with upward adjustments to protein, selenium, zinc, iron, and B vitamins to reflect silkie chicken's documented higher mineral density, and minor carbohydrate/sugar additions from wolfberries and red dates. No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for double-boiled silkie chicken soup are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo as of 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in available approved sources. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on broth-based chicken soups in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. Glycemic load per 300 ml bowl calculated as (35 × 4.5 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.575, rounded to 1.8 (using 5.1g total available carbohydrate per bowl at 300g serving). 4. The TCM 'qi-restoring' claim is not supported by RCT evidence and is not reflected in the health_context guidance. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301521 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are used as closest-parent matches; no silkie-specific or double-boiled preparation codes exist in either ontology as of 2024. 6. Halal status is based on the dish containing only chicken, wolfberries, red dates, and water with no pork or alcohol ingredients; individual preparation may vary.
